coredata.dc:description: Malachite green, known for its antifungal and antibacterial properties, poses health and environmental risks, necessitating effective detection methods. To address this, a system combining molecularly imprinted polymers with red-emissive carbon dots (r-CDs@MIPs) was developed for the adsorption and detection of malachite green. The system demonstrated a high adsorption capacity (108.8 mg/g), following the Langmuir model, indicating uniform adsorption sites. Kinetic studies showed rapid adsorption equilibrium within 40 min. Fluorescence tests revealed a strong, linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and malachite green concentration (0.014–300 μM), confirming high sensitivity. The sensor was successfully applied in fish sample analysis with recovery rates of 97.5 % to 104.4 % and low relative standard deviations (2.65 %–3.54 %). These findings affirm the sensor’s precision and reliability for real-world use. The r-CDs@MIPs probe offers a promising solution for detecting malachite green in food and environmental samples, advancing food safety and environmental monitoring techniques.

head.ce:title: A novel red emissive glutathione-capped carbon dots embedded within molecularly-imprinted polymers for adsorption and fluorescent sensing of malachite green in food samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 Steps for preparation of r-CDs@MIP for adsorption and detection of malachite green.

Malachite green, known for its antifungal and antibacterial properties, poses health and environmental risks, necessitating effective detection methods. To address this, a system combining molecularly imprinted polymers with red-emissive carbon dots (r-CDs@MIPs) was developed for the adsorption and detection of malachite green. The system demonstrated a high adsorption capacity (108.8 mg/g), following the Langmuir model, indicating uniform adsorption sites. Kinetic studies showed rapid adsorption equilibrium within 40 min. Fluorescence tests revealed a strong, linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and malachite green concentration (0.014–300 μM), confirming high sensitivity. The sensor was successfully applied in fish sample analysis with recovery rates of 97.5 % to 104.4 % and low relative standard deviations (2.65 %–3.54 %). These findings affirm the sensor’s precision and reliability for real-world use. The r-CDs@MIPs probe offers a promising solution for detecting malachite green in food and environmental samples, advancing food safety and environmental monitoring techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring food safety is a critical public health issue that significantly affects nations worldwide, regardless of their level of development [1]. Unsafe food can lead to numerous health issues, making it crucial to identify and eliminate harmful substances from food and its production resources to maintain safety standards [2,3]. While numerous nanomaterials have been created to reduce the exposures posed by harmful compounds in the food, there has been limited exploration of multifunctional materials capable of both detecting and facilitating the in-situ removal of these contaminants [4]. Furthermore, achieving multi-target detection with a single sensor remains a significant challenge from a sensing standpoint [5].

Malachite green is widely used in the aquaculture and fishery sectors to protect aquatic organisms from parasitic and fungal infections [6]. Additionally, it is frequently employed in fish transfer to extend the lifespan of fish with scale damage, owing to its potent antifungal effects [7]. Malachite green is widespread and highly toxic to both terrestrial and aquatic organisms. It can also have teratogenic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic effects on humans [8]. As a result, the use of malachite green has been prohibited in many countries. However, the illegal use of malachite green is due to low cost and the lack of equally effective alternatives. Therefore, precise detection of malachite green in aquatic products and water systems is crucial for ensuring food safety and protecting human health. Although various methods, including HPLC, GC/MS, SERS, and LC/MS, have been designed for the detection of malachite green [9–12], a simple, rapid, and sensitive detection method for malachite green remains underexplored. In contrast, fluorescent sensing has garnered significant research interest due to its high selectivity/sensitivity, quick response time, and the ability for visual detection [13–15].

Molecular imprinting has emerged as a versatile and efficient technique for creating synthetic receptors that possess specific molecular-recognition sites [16–20]. The adaptable chemistry of imprinted polymers enables the incorporation of various fluorophore recognition units, allowing for the generation of valuable results by combining multiple outputs [21]. Recently, a significant number of fluorescent molecularly-imprinted polymers (MIPs) incorporating nanoclusters, carbon quantum dots, semiconductor quantum dots, and graphene quantum dots have been developed, demonstrating promising potential for a wide range of applications in optical sensing [22–25]. Additionally, various MIPs have been developed for the adsorption and detection of malachite green. For instance, Zhang et al. developed core–shell MIPs doped with CdTe quantum dots and coated silica nanoparticles for detecting malachite green, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.7 nmol/L [26]. Duan et al. fabricated metal–organic frameworks capped with an aptamer and MIPs for malachite green detection, achieving an LOD of 0.1 ng/mL [27]. Wang et al. optimized a solid-phase adsorption MIP for malachite green adsorption, followed by a colorimetric method, attaining an LOD of 0.1 μg/L [28]. Zhang et al prepared a MIP for adsorption of the dye followed by surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) measurement [29]. Lin et al. fabricated magnetic MIPs for the rapid adsorption of malachite green from fish samples [30]. Awokoya et al. developed a conjugate-imprinted adsorbent by incorporating a trimethoprim-vanillin ligand into a highly crosslinked polymer, designed for the efficient removal of toxic organic dyes from wastewater [31]. Zhao et al. synthesized gold nanoclusters coated on MIP-based covalent organic frameworks for the selective detection of malachite green, achieving LOD of 2.78 nM [32].

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained more attentions in various fields as a result of their fascinating optical, physical, and chemical characteristics [33,34]. They have several advantages including water solubility, high fluorescence quantum yield, biocompatibility, low cost, and non-toxicity [35–37]. The synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots (r-CDs) is challenging because most CDs typically emit in the blue or green region [38–40]. The fabrication of red- and yellow-emitting CDs remains challenging due to quantum confinement effects, limited π-conjugation, surface defect control, excitation-dependent emission, and aggregation-induced quenching [41,42]. Achieving stable, reproducible, and high-quantum-yield red/yellow CDs requires precise structural engineering, optimized doping strategies, and controlled synthesis conditions, which are still areas of active research in nanomaterials science.

Dye molecules, as aromatic small molecules with functional groups, serve as effective precursors for synthesizing long-wavelength CDs. Their conjugated electronic structure can be partially retained during synthesis, enabling the production of red-emitting CDs with enhanced stability and eco-friendliness while preserving the excitation-emission properties of the dye [43,44]. Notably, neutral red, a phenazine dye with multiple aromatic rings and double bonds, is ideal for this purpose. Its optical properties are highly pH-sensitive, making it valuable for sensing applications. During synthesis, partial carbonization forms the carbon core, while uncarbonized portions integrate into the CDs, influencing their final optical properties and environmental responsiveness [45–47]. Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide containing thiol (−SH), carboxyl (–COOH), and amine (–NH2) groups, which significantly improves quantum yield and fluorescence stability. The −SH groups in GSH help in surface passivation, reducing non-radiative recombination and enhancing photoluminescence [48,49]. Moreover, the incorporation of −SH- allows for better surface modification, providing negative charges (−SH and –COOH groups) that enhance selectivity toward positively charged analytes such as malachite green [50].

The study modifies and extends the solvothermal method to create a novel hybrid material by integrating r-CDs into MIPs. This is the first reported combination of red-emissive, thiol-modified CDs synthesized via solvothermal methods with MIPs, yielding a highly selective, sensitive, and stable dual-functional system for malachite green detection.

Malachite green (as chloride salt, 98.7 %), red neutral (98.6 %), glucose (97.8 %), glutathione (GSH, 98.4 %), cysteine (98.5 %), lemon yellow (99.6 %), Congo red (98.4 %), sunset yellow (97.7 %), curcumin (97.8 %), tartrazine (99.3 %), crystal violet (97.8 %), ascorbic acid (97.6 %), uric acid (97.4 %), dopamine (99.3 %), and sudan I (98.4 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (AR grade), ethanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR grade), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), quinine sulfate, EDTA, azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), glycidyl methacrylate (GYMA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), triethylamine, 2-bromoisobutyl bromide (Br-isoBut), Bipyridine (BPY), 4-vinylpyridine (4-VinyP), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), copper (I) bromide (CuBr), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDGMA), tetrahydrofurn (THF), cyclohexanol, and disodium hydrogen phosphate (AR grade) were obtained from Merck. Deionized water (DIW) consumed in whole experiments was obtained from a Milli-Q system.

Fluorescence and absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) and a Shimadzu UV-1601 PC spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) was carried out using JEOL JSM-5400 LV instrument (Oxford, USA). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analyses were performed using a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with an AXIS ULTRA DLD system (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray powder diffraction (PXRD) patterns of the R-CDs were acquired using a PW 1710 X-ray diffractometer (Philips-FEI, Netherlands). Differential Scanning Calorimetry/Thermogravimetric Analysis (DSC/TGA) of the samples was carried out with a Universal V4.5-TA Instruments (USA), with a rate of 10 °C min−1.

The preparation of r-CDs was carried out following these procedures under optimized conditions. Briefly, neutral red (500 mg) was combined with GSH (356 mg) and dissolved in 40 mL of 30 % ethanol. Sulfuric acid (0.3 mL) was then added gradually to the mixture. After stirring for five minutes, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 8 h. Once cooled, the product was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 30 min, followed by dialysis using a membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 2000 Da for 24 h. The resulting red-fluorescent filtrate was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

A precisely weighed amount of PVP (1.65 g) was mixed with 15 mL of GYMA and dissolved in 75 mL of ethanol using ultrasonication for 15 min. Subsequently, 0.4 g of AIBN was added to the solution, followed by N2 purging for 20 min to remove dissolved oxygen. The mixture was then subjected to rotary evaporation at 80 °C for 36 h. The resulting product was stored in a 0.35 % SDS solution.
The preparation follows report [51] with some modifications and the main steps for preparation of poly(GYMA-co-EDGMA) (termed as MAR) were summarized in the supporting information part.

Surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization was employed to synthesize fluorescent-based MIPs. In brief, 0.85 g of MAR-Br, 105 µL of 4-VinyP, 70 mg of malachite green, 255 µL of EDGMA, 30 mg of BPY, and 100 µL of concentrated r-CDs were sequentially dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol. Under a nitrogen atmosphere, 25 mg of CuBr was promptly added to the mixture. The reaction was conducted at 70 °C for 10 h. The resulting product was sequentially washed with EDTA, water, and ethanol. Malachite green was removed via 15 mL ethanol: acetic acid mixture (8:2, v/v) for 20 min. After drying, the final r-CDs@MIP product was obtained and prepared for further characterization. The non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was synthesized following the same procedure, except that malachite green was omitted from the synthesis process.

In the isothermal adsorption study, r-CDs@MIP was introduced into solutions with varying malachite green concentrations. The experimental setup involved preparing 100 mL solutions of malachite green at concentrations of 2, 5, 8, 15, 20, 35, 45, 50, 60, 70, and 85 mg/L in 250 mL conical flasks. Each flask received 12.0 mg of r-CDs@MIP. The mixtures were shaken at a constant temperature at 220 rpm for 3 h at room temperature. After the shaking process, the suspensions were centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to collect the malachite green solution after adsorption. The adsorption amount of malachite green can be calculated from the following formula:
(1) Qe= (C0-Ce) × V/m

Where Qe is the equilibrium adsorption capacity in mg g−1 while C0 and Ce stand for initial concentration and equilibrium concentration, respectively. V and m stand for volume of aqueous phase (mL) and amount of material used (mg), respectively. Moreover, the isothermal adsorption data were further illustrated using Langmuir and Freundlich models as follows:
(2) Langmuir model: Ce/Qe=Ce/Qmax + 1/KLQmax
(3) Freundlich model: lnQe = lnCe/n + lnKF

Ce: Equilibrium concentration of the adsorbate in solution (mg/L); Qe: Amount of adsorbate adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium (mg/g); Qmax: Maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent (mg/g); KL: Langmuir constant related to the affinity of the adsorbate for the adsorbent (ml/mg); n: Empirical parameter indicating adsorption intensity or heterogeneity of the surface; KF: Freundlich constant representing adsorption capacity (mg/g).

The dynamic adsorption experiment utilized a 100 mL solution of malachite green at a concentration of 40 mg/L in a conical flask. To this solution, 12.0 mg of r-CDs@MIP were added, and the mixture was agitated at a constant temperature at 220 rpm for varying time intervals (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60, 80, 100, 130, 160, 200, and 260 min). Following agitation, the suspension was centrifuged and passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to isolate the malachite green solution after adsorption. Furthermore, the dynamic adsorption data were studied using pseudo-first order kinetic model as follows:
(3) ln(Qe-Qt) = lnQe-K1t

Qe: Amount of solute adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/g); Qt: Amount of solute adsorbed at time t (mg/g); k1: Rate constant for the pseudo-first-order reaction (1/min); t: Time (min). This equation is used to determine the rate constant k1 by plotting ln(Qe − Qt) (y-axis) against t (x-axis). The slope of the line is − k1 while the y-intercept is lnQe.

Moreover, pseudo-second order was used to describe dynamic adsorption data as follows:
(4) t/Qt = t/Qe + 1/K2Qe2

Qe: Amount of solute adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/g); Qt: Amount of solute adsorbed at time t (mg/g); k2: Rate constant for the pseudo-second-order reaction (g/mg•min); t: Time (min). This equation is used to determine Qe and k2 by plotting t/Qt (y-axis) against t (x-axis). The slope of the line is 1/Qe. The y-intercept is 1/k2Q2 e.

A 1.5 mL solution of r-CDs or r-CDs@MIP (or r-CDs@NIP) at a concentration of 0.65 mg/mL was mixed with 0.5 mL malachite green solutions of varying concentrations. The mixtures were gently agitated for 5 min at room temperature to ensure adequate interaction between the adsorbent and the dye. Subsequently, the fluorescence emissions were measured at 640 nm following excitation at 330 nm. This process facilitated the evaluation of the fluorescence response of the materials in detecting malachite green.

The performance of the proposed extraction method was evaluated using two fish species: crucian carp and grass carp. Fish samples were triturated, and 3 g of tissue were mixed with 15 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.2) in a 50 mL centrifuge tube. The mixture was homogenized and subjected to sonication for 20 min at room temperature to enhance extraction efficiency. Following sonication, the homogenate was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min, and the resulting supernatant was collected. A 1.5 mL aliquot of the filtrate was mixed with varying amounts of malachite green, and the sample was passed through a self-fabricated solid-phase extraction column packed with r-CDs@MIPs. To remove impurities and interfering species, the column was washed with deionized water. Malachite green was then eluted completely using 15 mL of an ethanol: acetic acid mixture (8:2, v/v). The eluate was evaporated under a N2 stream, and the residue was reconstituted in 0.2 mL of methanol for fluorometric analysis.

Fig. 1
A illustrates the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as-synthesized r-CDs, showcasing their spherical morphology and uniform distribution. The inset presents the size distribution of the r-CDs, with an average diameter of 4.6 nm, calculated based on a sample size of 70 nanoparticles (n = 70), as depicted in Fig. 1 B. Fig. 1 C provides a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, revealing the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which is consistent with a graphite-like crystalline structure, confirming the carbonaceous nature of the r-CDs [52]. Furthermore, the surface morphology of the r-CDs after modification was examined using SEM. Fig. S1A and S1B depict the surface characteristics of r-CDs@MIP and r-CDs@NIP, respectively. The distinct surface patterns observed between the r-CDs@MIP and r-CDs@NIP suggest successful functionalization, with potential implications for enhanced selectivity and performance in targeted applications.

The surface functional groups of r-CDs, MAR-Br, and r-CDs@MIP nanomaterials were analyzed using FTIR, as shown in Fig. S2. The FTIR spectrum of r-CDs reveals characteristic absorption bands at 3376 cm−1, 2830–2950 cm−1, 2420 cm−1, 1615 cm−1, and 1510 cm−1, corresponding to υ (OH/NH), υ (CH), υ (SH), υ (C=O), and δ (OH/NH) vibrations, respectively. These bands confirm the presence of hydroxyl, amine, thiol, and carbonyl groups on the surface of r-CDs. For MAR-Br, the FTIR spectrum exhibits absorption bands at 3276 cm−1, 2840–2930 cm−1, 1695 cm−1, and 1512 cm−1, attributed to υ (OH), υ (CH), υ (C=O), and δ (OH), respectively, which are indicative of its functional composition [53,54]. The FTIR spectrum of r-CDs@MIP retained the absorption bands characteristic of both r-CDs and MAR-Br, indicating the successful integration of MAR-Br onto the surface of r-CDs during the fabrication process. This suggests that the functional groups essential for molecular imprinting were effectively preserved, supporting the structural integrity and potential application of r-CDs@MIP in targeted chemical recognition.

The elemental composition and surface functional groups of r-CDs, MAR-Br, and r-CDs@MIP nanomaterials were further characterized using XPS, as depicted in Fig. S3. Fig. S3 A presents the XPS survey spectra of the three materials, highlighting their elemental compositions. The XPS spectrum of r-CDs reveals the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S), confirming the incorporation of these elements during synthesis. Notably, the nitrogen and sulfur contents are attributed to the GSH used as a precursor in the r-CDs synthesis, providing functionality essential for their applications. The detailed XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p for r-CDs are presented in Fig. S3 B–3E, providing insights into their chemical bonding and surface functionalities. Fig. S3 B shows the deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum, featuring distinct peaks at binding energies of 284.5 eV and 285.8 eV. These peaks correspond to C=C and C-C/C-N bonds, respectively, confirming the sp2 hybridized carbon framework and functionalized carbon components in the r-CDs. Fig. S3 C illustrates the deconvoluted N 1 s spectrum, with prominent peaks at 400.6 eV, 404.7 eV, and 408.9 eV. These peaks are attributed to pyridine-like nitrogen, pyrrole-like nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen, respectively, indicating the incorporation of diverse nitrogen functionalities within the r-CDs structure. Fig. S3 D explores the deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum, showing peaks at 531.8 eV, 534.7 eV, and 537.6 eV. These peaks correspond to C-O, C=O, and C-OH/C-O-C groups, respectively, highlighting the oxygen-rich functionalities on the r-CDs surface, essential for their hydrophilicity and chemical reactivity. Finally, Fig. S3 E presents the deconvoluted S 2p spectrum, with sharp peaks observed at 161.4 eV and 169.8 eV, assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively. These peaks confirm the presence of sulfur-based groups, further validating the role of glutathione as a sulfur source during synthesis. The detailed XPS analysis underscores the diverse functional groups and structural heterogeneity of r-CDs, which are crucial for their performance in targeted applications such as sensing, catalysis, or molecular recognition.

Furthermore, the crystalline structure of the r-CDs was investigated using XRD analysis, as shown in Fig. S4. The XRD pattern of r-CDs exhibits a prominent sharp peak at 23.2°, which corresponds to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon. This finding aligns well with the HRTEM analysis, confirming the graphitic-like structure of the r-CDs. Interestingly, a similar XRD pattern was observed for r-CDs incorporated into the r-CDs@MIP. This indicates that the integration process did not alter the position or shape of the characteristic graphitic-like peak. The preservation of the structural integrity of r-CDs within the MIP matrix further highlights the compatibility and stability of the r-CDs during the fabrication process. The combination of XRD and HRTEM analyses provides robust evidence for the retention of the graphitic carbon structure, which is critical for the functional performance of r-CDs in advanced applications such as sensing and molecular recognition.

Fig. S5 presents the thermogravimetric curve (TGA) of r-CDs where it exhibits two thermal events. The first initial loss between 30–120 °C is attributed to the loss of water on the surface of r-CDs (13.89 % weight loss). The second major loss between 150–380 °C is attributed to the thermal degradation of organic functional groups (such as GSH), leading to significant weight loss (85.87 % weight loss). This corresponds to the breaking of C–N, C–O, and C–S bonds. The carbon core of the dots remains relatively stable, with minimal further degradation beyond 600 °C, indicating the presence of graphitic carbon structures. Moreover, the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) indicates the presence of endothermic and exothermic peaks at approximately 93 °C and 188 °C, corresponding to evaporation of water and decomposition of the GSH shell, respectively. The absence of major exothermic peaks beyond this point suggests the stability of the carbonized core.

The spectroscopic characteristics of the as-fabricated r-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 2
, showcasing their optical properties. Fig. 2 A presents the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the r-CDs, which features two prominent absorption bands at 260 nm and 393 nm. These bands are attributed to π-π* transitions of the aromatic C=C bonds and n-π* transitions of C=N/C=O groups, respectively, reflecting the unique electronic structure of r-CDs. The third band is typically associated with surface states or functional groups introduced during synthesis (neutral red). Fig. 2 B displays the excitation and fluorescence spectra of r-CDs, with excitation and emission wavelengths observed at 330 nm and 640 nm, respectively. This broad fluorescence emission highlights the potential of r-CDs for applications in bioimaging and sensing. The emission behavior of r-CDs was further investigated by monitoring the fluorescence spectra at varying excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 2 C. The results reveal a red-shift and an increase in emission intensity with increasing excitation wavelengths within the range of 280–360 nm. This excitation-dependent emission behavior is characteristic of r-CDs and is attributed to the distribution of surface states and heterogeneity in the electronic environment of r-CDs. These spectroscopic properties underline the versatility of r-CDs in optoelectronic and photoluminescent applications, offering significant potential for advanced material and biological research.

The stability of the as-prepared r-CDs was evaluated under various environmental conditions, as depicted in Fig. 3. The r-CDs demonstrated a high degree of stability across a range of testing parameters, showcasing their robustness for potential applications. Specifically, the r-CDs maintained their structural and optical properties in NaCl solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 180 mM, indicating their resistance to ionic strength variations. The photostability of r-CDs was evaluated by continuously exposing the samples to the light source for duration of 300 min. Fluorescence intensity was recorded at regular intervals (every 30 min) to monitor potential degradation. A 365 nm UV-LED lamp with a power density of 10 mW/cm2 was used as the excitation source during the experiment. Moreover, the stability of r-CDs was also assessed across a wide pH range (3–10), where minimal variations in fluorescence intensity were observed, highlighting their adaptability to different acidic and basic environments. Additionally, the r-CDs retained their stability at elevated temperatures, with consistent performance observed in the temperature range of 25–85 °C. The fluorescence emissions of r-CDs remained unaffected after 28 days of storage. These findings collectively underscore the resilience of r-CDs under diverse conditions, making them suitable for applications in challenging environments, such as biomedical imaging, sensing, and environmental monitoring. The quantum yield (QY) of the r-CDs was determined to quantify their fluorescence efficiency. Using a comparative method with a standard reference (quinine sulfate), the QY was calculated to be 34.78 %. This value highlights the excellent photoluminescent properties of the r-CDs, making them suitable for applications in bioimaging and optoelectronic devices. Detailed calculations and methodologies are provided in the Supporting Information

The mechanisms underlying photoluminescence quenching in carbon dots (CDs) are often attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), dynamic quenching, and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [42,55]. Fig. S6A reveals a significant overlap between the absorption spectrum of malachite green and the emission spectrum of r-CDs, indicating the potential involvement of IFE or FRET in the quenching process. Given that the electrostatic interactions between the positively charged malachite green and the negatively charged r-CDs (surface functionalized with –SH groups) typically result in a separation distance exceeding 10 nm, the presence of FRET is strongly supported [56]. Fig. S6B provides evidence for the electrostatic interactions between r-CDs and malachite green, as demonstrated by the zeta potential measurements. The zeta potentials of r-CDs, malachite green, and the r-CDs + malachite green were determined to be –32.78 mV, +4.56 mV, and –22.56 mV, respectively. This shift in zeta potential confirms the interaction between the negatively charged surface groups (e.g., –SH) of r-CDs and the positively charged malachite green. To further elucidate the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime measurements of r-CDs were performed before and after the addition of malachite green, as shown in Fig. S6C. The fluorescence lifetime of r-CDs decreased from 6.34 ns to 6.12 ns following the addition of malachite green. This reduction in lifetime is consistent with the FRET mechanism, where the donor (r-CDs) transfers energy to the acceptor (malachite green), leading to a decrease in fluorescence lifetime. In contrast, the IFE is characterized by a reduction in excitation light due to absorbance by the acceptor, with the donor's fluorescence lifetime remaining largely unaffected [57]. Fig. S6D illustrates the UV/Vis absorption spectra of r-CDs before and after the addition of malachite green. A notable increase in the absorption bands of r-CDs was observed upon the addition of malachite green, indicative of fluorescence quenching. Importantly, no new absorption bands were detected after the interaction, suggesting that the quenching mechanism is primarily dynamic in nature [58]. This aligns with the characteristics of dynamic quenching, where collisional interactions or transient complexes between the quencher and the fluorophore dominate, without altering the electronic states of the system. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of r-CDs upon the addition of malachite green can be attributed to a combination of FRET and dynamic quenching mechanisms. The presence of FRET is supported by the spectral overlap and reduction in fluorescence lifetime, while the dynamic quenching component is consistent with the changes in absorption observed in the UV/Vis spectra. These dual quenching processes highlight the intricate photophysical interactions between r-CDs and malachite green, offering valuable insights for the design of sensitive and efficient fluorescence-based sensors.

The fluorometric probe developed was employed for the detection of malachite green in standard solutions. The results revealed that increasing concentrations of malachite green led to a notable quenching of the fluorescence emission of r-CDs (Fig. 4
A). The fluorescence intensity was measured both before (F0) and after (F) incubation with malachite green. A plot illustrating the relationship between F0/F and malachite green concentration within the range of 0.014 to 300 µM was constructed (Fig. 4 B). The data exhibited a linear correlation, expressed by the regression equation F0/F = 1.02 + 0.0031[malachite green] (R2 = 0.9964). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, yielding a value of 0.0012 µM. These findings demonstrate the probe's high sensitivity and potential utility in detecting trace levels of malachite green, offering a robust analytical method for environmental applications. The analytical performance of the proposed system for malachite green detection was evaluated and compared with other reported systems (Table 1 ). The results indicate that the proposed system offers a notably low LOD and a broad linear detection range, highlighting its superior sensitivity and versatility.

The degree of adsorption onto the template material is a key parameter for evaluating the performance of adsorbents, which was determined through saturation adsorption experiments. As shown in Fig. S7A, the isothermal adsorption curve exhibits a clear trend of increasing adsorption capacity for malachite green across a concentration range of 2–85 mg/L. The adsorption process eventually reached a state of saturation equilibrium at a malachite green concentration of 45 mg/L, indicating the completion of the adsorption process. The maximum adsorption capacities for r-CDs@MIP and MIP were found to be 118.3 mg/g and 117.4 mg/g, respectively. These values significantly exceeded the adsorption capacity of the NIP, which demonstrated a considerably lower adsorption capacity of 35.8 mg/g. This enhanced adsorption performance of r-CDs@MIP and MIP suggests the importance of functionalization and the optimization of template interactions in improving adsorption efficacy.

The superior adsorption performance observed for r-CDs@MIP and MIP can be attributed to the presence of imprinted cavities within these materials, which were specifically engineered to selectively recognize malachite green molecules. In contrast, NIP contained non-imprinted cavities, resulting in significantly reduced performance. Additionally, MIP and r-CDs@MIP demonstrated specific binding affinity for malachite green, as evidenced by imprinting factor (IF) values of 3.3 and 3.27, respectively. The imprinting factor (IF), defined as the ratio of saturated adsorption between MIP and NIP, is an indicator of the enhanced selective binding capacity of MIP over NIP [68]. The adsorption kinetic of malachite green was further investigated through kinetic adsorption experiments, with the resulting adsorption curves presented in Fig. S7B. These data revealed a rapid increase in malachite green adsorption during the first 10 min, followed by a plateau at equilibrium after approximately 40 min. The saturation adsorption capacities for r-CDs@MIP and MIP were 50.2 mg/g and 52.3 mg/g, respectively, whereas NIP achieved a much lower capacity of 15.4 mg/g. The markedly lower adsorption capacity of NIP is primarily attributed to the absence of imprinted cavities, which allowed r-CDs@MIP and MIP to bind malachite green more efficiently and rapidly. The gradual decline in the adsorption rate over time is likely due to the progressive occupation of available binding sites, leading to a reduction in the adsorption rate as the surface cavities become increasingly occupied.

To elucidate the adsorption mechanism of malachite green on the adsorbents, isothermal adsorption data were fitted to both the Langmuir and Freundlich models. The results, summarized in Table S1 and Fig. S8, demonstrated excellent linear correlation coefficients for the Langmuir model: 0.9931, 0.9988, and 0.9843 for r-CDs@MIP, MIP, and r-CDs@NIP, respectively. In contrast, the Freundlich model yielded slightly lower coefficients of 0.8853, 0.9076, and 0.9528 for r-CDs@MIP, MIP, and r-CDs@NIP, respectively. These results suggest that the adsorption process of malachite green on r-CDs@MIP, MIP, and r-CDs@NIP is better described by the Langmuir model. The Langmuir model assumes adsorption occurs in a monolayer on a homogeneous surface with a finite number of adsorption sites, which corresponds to a well-defined maximum adsorption capacity. This suggests that the adsorption sites on the adsorbents are energetically uniform and that the adsorption is likely governed by the availability of these sites, rather than by multilayer adsorption or surface heterogeneity.

To further explore the adsorption kinetics mechanism, the kinetic adsorption curves were analyzed using both the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models. The pseudo-first-order model generally assumes that adsorption rate is controlled by the diffusion process, while the pseudo-second-order model suggests that chemical forces are the key factors influencing the rate of adsorption. The linear correlation coefficients for the pseudo-first-order model were as follows: r-CDs@MIP (0.9559), MIP (0.9749), and NIP (0.9834). For the pseudo-second-order model, the coefficients were r-CDs@MIP (0.9986), MIP (0.9987), and NIP (0.9981), as shown in Table S2 and Fig. S9. These results indicate that the adsorbents adhered more closely to the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, which is indicative of a chemical adsorption process.

The proposed fluorometric probe was evaluated for its efficacy in detecting malachite green in crucian and grass carp samples, both before and after spiking with the analyte. The method demonstrated excellent recovery rates, ranging from 97.5 % to 104.4 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 2.65 % and 3.54 % (Table 2 ). Additionally, the results obtained from the fish sample analysis were cross-validated using the standard HPLC/UV method [69]. The comparable outcomes between the two methods underscore the reliability and accuracy of the proposed probe for detecting malachite green in aquatic samples.

This study presents the development of a red fluorescent carbon dot-based material (r-CDs) with a quenching effect on malachite green, synthesized and integrated into microspheres modified with a MIP through surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization. The resulting r-CDs@MIP material offers several notable advantages. Firstly, the adsorption performance of r-CDs@MIP under identical conditions was comparable to that of the MIP and significantly exceeded that of the non-imprinted polymer (NIP), highlighting its superior specificity. Secondly, r-CDs@MIP exhibited excellent fluorescent detection capabilities for malachite green, showing a robust linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and malachite green concentration within a wide range of 0.014–300 μM. Thirdly, the material demonstrated effective enrichment capabilities, enhancing its applicability for trace analysis. In conclusion, r-CDs@MIP successfully combines the high specificity and selective adsorption properties of molecular imprinting technology with the fluorescence functionality of carbon dots. This dual-functional material enables simultaneous enrichment and detection of malachite green in food samples, offering a promising approach for food safety and environmental monitoring applications. Its integration of molecular imprinting and photoluminescence paves the way for significant advancements in analytical methods for contaminants detection.
